title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
Phase II archaeological evaluation is a critical step in the cultural resource management (CRM) process, determining whether a site discovered during Phase I survey is significant enough to warrant protection or further study. For federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers working in the Southeast US, understanding Phase II evaluation helps ensure regulatory compliance and informed decision-making before construction begins.

Quick Answer: Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted excavation study that tests a previously identified site to determine whether it meets the National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. Archaeologists collect systematic data to assess a site's integrity, significance, and research potential before any federal undertaking moves forward.

What Triggers a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that may be significant under the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) criteria. The lead federal agency, in consultation with the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO), determines whether that site requires additional testing before a finding of effect can be made.

Under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA), federal agencies must consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties. If Phase I field work uncovers artifacts, features, or other indicators of a potentially significant site within a project's area of potential effects (APE), Phase II evaluation becomes the next required step.

Common triggers include high artifact densities, intact stratigraphic deposits, structural features like hearths or post molds, or the presence of human remains. State DOTs, utility companies, and developers working with federal permits—such as Section 404 Army Corps permits—frequently encounter this requirement. Early identification keeps projects on schedule and avoids costly surprises later in construction.

How Is Phase II Evaluation Different From Phase I Survey?

Phase II archaeological evaluation goes far deeper—literally and analytically—than Phase I survey. While Phase I identifies the presence and general boundaries of sites through shovel tests and surface collection, Phase II uses more intensive excavation methods to assess a site's eligibility for listing on the National Register of Historic Places.

During Phase I, archaeologists typically excavate shovel test pits on a systematic grid, often 30- to 50-meter intervals, to locate sites across a broad project corridor. Phase II narrows that focus to a specific site, using hand-excavated test units—commonly 1×1 or 1×2 meter squares—to expose intact cultural deposits, document stratigraphy, and recover a statistically meaningful artifact assemblage.

Phase II also involves more thorough laboratory analysis. Artifacts are catalogued, analyzed by material type and temporal period, and interpreted in context. Radiocarbon dating, faunal analysis, botanical sampling, and sediment studies may all be employed. The goal is answering four key NRHP eligibility questions: Is the site associated with important events? Important persons? Does it embody distinctive construction methods? Or does it have the potential to yield important information? Most archaeological sites qualify—or don't—under that last criterion, Criterion D.

What Methods Do Archaeologists Use During Phase II?

Phase II archaeological evaluation relies on controlled, systematic excavation combined with rigorous laboratory and archival research. Archaeologists design a data recovery strategy tailored to the specific site type, research questions, and preservation conditions at hand.

Typical field methods include hand-excavated 1×1 meter units placed in a stratified random or judgmental sample across the site. All sediment is screened through ¼-inch wire mesh—sometimes finer for specialized studies—to ensure thorough artifact recovery. Features such as pits, hearths, and structural remains are carefully exposed, mapped, photographed, and sampled before removal.

GIS and remote sensing technologies increasingly support Phase II work. Ground-penetrating radar (GPR), magnetometry, and resistivity surveys can identify subsurface features without excavation, helping archaeologists target their test units more efficiently. Drone photogrammetry creates precise site maps that document spatial relationships between features and artifacts. After fieldwork, laboratory analysis generates the quantitative and qualitative data needed to write the evaluation report and support a defensible NRHP eligibility recommendation to the SHPO and federal agency.

What Happens After Phase II Evaluation Is Complete?

After Phase II archaeological evaluation is complete, the consulting archaeologist prepares a technical report presenting the findings, methods, and a formal recommendation on NRHP eligibility. This report is submitted to the lead federal agency and the SHPO for review and concurrence.

If the site is determined not eligible for the NRHP, the project may proceed through that portion of the APE with no further archaeological constraints, provided other regulatory requirements are met. If the site is determined eligible, the Section 106 process continues with an assessment of adverse effect. A finding of adverse effect—meaning the project will harm the site's integrity—typically leads to a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) outlining mitigation measures.

The most common mitigation for an eligible archaeological site is Phase III data recovery excavation, sometimes called full-scale excavation or mitigation. This involves excavating a statistically significant portion of the site to recover the information that makes it significant before it is destroyed. In some cases, project redesign to avoid the site entirely is preferable and should always be considered first. Avoidance preserves the resource in place and can eliminate the need for Phase III work altogether, saving both time and money.

How Long Does Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Take?

The timeline for Phase II archaeological evaluation varies depending on site size, complexity, access conditions, and agency review schedules. For a moderately sized prehistoric site, fieldwork may take one to two weeks, with laboratory analysis and report preparation adding six to twelve weeks more.

Larger or more complex sites—multi-component occupations, historic-period structural remains, or sites with abundant faunal and botanical material requiring specialized analysis—can extend the process considerably. Specialized dating methods like AMS radiocarbon dating typically add four to eight weeks for laboratory turnaround, and SHPO review periods vary by state, commonly running 30 to 45 days.

Early coordination between the project proponent, the federal agency, and the CRM firm is the best way to keep Phase II evaluation on a realistic schedule. Submitting a well-designed research design for SHPO and agency comment before fieldwork begins prevents costly scope revisions after the fact. Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research works closely with project teams to build Phase II timelines into project schedules from the outset, minimizing delays and keeping federal undertakings moving forward.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the purpose of Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Phase II archaeological evaluation determines whether a previously identified site meets the National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. Using systematic excavation and laboratory analysis, archaeologists assess site integrity, significance, and research potential to inform Section 106 compliance decisions for federal undertakings.

Who requires Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Federal agencies and their applicants or licensees are required to conduct Phase II evaluation under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act when Phase I survey identifies a potentially significant site within a project's area of potential effects. State DOTs, utility companies, and private developers with federal permits commonly encounter this requirement.

How much does Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Phase II archaeological evaluation costs vary widely based on site size, complexity, access, and required analyses. Small single-component sites may range from $15,000 to $40,000, while larger or more complex evaluations can exceed $100,000. Specialized laboratory analyses, remote sensing studies, and extended SHPO review can add to the total.

Can a project avoid Phase II evaluation?

A project can avoid Phase II evaluation by redesigning its footprint to completely avoid the site identified during Phase I survey. If the site falls entirely outside the revised area of potential effects, no further evaluation is required. Avoidance is often the fastest and most cost-effective path forward when site significance is uncertain.

What is the difference between Phase II and Phase III archaeology?

Phase II evaluates whether a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. Phase III, or data recovery, is conducted only after a site is determined eligible and a project will adversely affect it. Phase III excavates a significant portion of the site to recover its important information before it is destroyed by construction.

Understanding Phase II archaeological evaluation is essential for any project team navigating federal CRM compliance in the Southeast. A well-executed evaluation protects your project timeline, satisfies regulatory requirements, and ensures that important cultural resources are identified and treated appropriately. Contact TVAR today to discuss your Phase II evaluation needs with our RPA-certified archaeologists.
excerpt: Learn what Phase II archaeological evaluation is, when it's required under Section 106, what methods archaeologists use, and what happens after the report is complete.
